results.title: Lattics - "Brain-like" Writing - App Store - Apple
results.content:
Lattics is designed for professionals in highly educated such as writers, art critics, bloggers, university students, scientific researchers, engineers, financial analysts etc, significantly improving the efficiency of writing novels, academic papers, research reports, project reports, and knowledge learning Lattics' design principles follow the divergent thinking and the structured info organization of the human brain, providing an immersive, multi-perspective, multi-dimensional, and interconnected info to stimulate creative efficiency. Users who have used Lattics generally believe that it far surpasses other similar software in both writing efficiency, quantity and quality of their work. Key Features: - Good Design: Excellent modern, minimalist, and stylish feel - AI-Assisted Writing: [...] Lattics introduces AI Assistence features for writing and deep research, including translation, proofreading, rewriting, creative writing, and Chat Research. - Supports multiple AI providers, including mainstream models from US and China, such as OpenAI, Antropic, Google, DeepSeek, GLM, Kimi, MiniMax, MiMo, etc., as well as third-party providers like OpenRouter and OpenCode. It also supports Ollama and user-deployed models. - Allows setting different AI models for different tools; for example, local models can be used for translation, while DeepSeek can be used for in-depth research. - Allows control and authorization of project folders accessed by AI, enhancing data privacy and security. - Allows editing of AI translation and rewriting results; AI can remember personal style and [...] yagmur.t

  I've been searching for an all-in-one note-taking app for years, trying all the usual suspects like Notion, Evernote, Obsidian, OneNote and literally 10 more others :). Honestly, this app is the one I've been waiting for which seems mimicking how the brain thinks (as a neuroscience enthusiast). I'm surprised it's not more popular and a bit underrated– I think it just needs a bit more polish to really shine. I'd love to see it on Kickstarter so I can support it. I'm definitely sticking with it and looking forward to seeing it grow and mature. Thanks

  Developer Response

  Hi, thanks for your appreciate, Please recommend it to your friends or fans, We have no plan on Kickstart, because we have done it for more than 3 years, step by step to grow up

results.title: What Is Lattics?
results.content:
Traditional tools make this worse. Files are isolated. Folders impose rigid hierarchies. Notes are either too structured (databases) or too loose (plain text). And when it's time to turn your research into a paper or report, you're starting from scratch — copying, pasting, and reorganizing everything manually.

There had to be a better way.

## Why "Brain-like"? A Design Philosophy, Not Just a Feature

Lattics isn't built around file systems or database schemas. It's built around a simple insight: your brain doesn't store knowledge in folders. It stores knowledge as a network of interconnected neurons, where each idea is linked to related ideas, and information is retrieved by association, not by navigating a directory tree. [...] Decades later, that vision remains largely unrealized. Most tools still organize knowledge like filing cabinets — not like brains. Lattics is our attempt to finally build the knowledge management system that Bush and Engelbart imagined: one that works with your mind, not against it.

## Start Thinking in Lattics

Free to start — no account required.

Free download for Windows

Also available for macOS

Or learn more on the pricing page.

Lattics - Brain-like Academic and Creative Writing

@ 2026Aura Marker Studio [...] This is the idea behind "brain-like" knowledge management. It's not about mimicking biology — it's about designing a tool that respects the way your mind actually works:

 Small, atomic units — like neurons, not files
 Rich connections — like synapses, not folders
 Flow-preserving design — protecting your limited working memory

The result is a tool that helps you capture, connect, and create knowledge — without getting in your way.

## Six Core Capabilities

### 1. Card-based Knowledge Storage

Instead of storing information in rigid files, Lattics uses cards — small, self-contained units of information. A card can hold a quote, an idea, a data point, a character description, or anything else. Cards live in a card library, where they can be freely organized, tagged, and searched.

results.title: Why Choose Lattics Over Obsidian? | Lattics Review
results.content:
Obsidian: At its core, it is an interconnected note-taking system. It acts like an infinitely expanding web of thoughts, encouraging you to discover new insights by creating connections between notes. Its strength lies in its high degree of freedom and customizability (through plugins), making it suitable for users who enjoy free exploration and building a personal "second brain."
 Lattics: It is positioned as a "Knowledge Base and Creative Writing App." It focuses not only on note association but also emphasizes providing complete support for long-form, structured creative projects (such as books, papers, and research reports). It is like a digital study, helping you to systematically build a complete work from scattered cards and ideas. [...] Lattics

January 8, 2026·6 min read

# Why Choose Lattics Over Obsidian?

Why Choose Lattics Over Obsidian?

When choosing knowledge management and writing tools, Obsidian has won the favor of a large number of users with its powerful bidirectional linking and a community ecosystem centered on plain text and local storage. However, the emerging tool Lattics is becoming a new choice for many creators, especially users engaged in in-depth research and structured writing, thanks to its unique positioning and feature set. This article will compare these two tools and explore in which scenarios Lattics might be a better alternative.

#### Core Positioning: Structured Creation vs. Free Association

results.title: Lattics | Its Function “Sucks”, But The Brain Helps
results.content:
Faced with these three views, I’m not quite sure if I’m operating correctly. If you’ve previously used Obsidian’s Canvas, Heptabase, or Scrintal, you might find Lattics confusing. You won’t clearly know which type of view you “should” be using, or if you are “correctly” using the current view.

## It Helps

Although Lattics may not be an excellent note-taking software, it is actually quite good for writing.

Lattics is particularly suitable for “traditional” people — those who have difficulty understanding new things or are reluctant to change their long-established writing habits using local software like Word.

Lattics still retains the retro love for magazines. In its document styles, it offers three magazine-style options: The Economist, Brooklyn, and Medium. [...] Therefore, my suggestion is to either completely revise the Block or delete it altogether, ensuring that its logic is consistent and aligns with contemporary mainstream note-taking software.

Mind Map

The logic behind the mind map feature also seems unclear. Unlike Heptabase, where we can quickly expand, edit, drag, and combine cards, Lattics requires us to switch between three modes:

1. Mind Map: Only allows editing of card titles.

2. Story View: Annotates the plot/summary of each card.

3. Bird’s Eye View: Adjusts the size of cards freely, facilitates card content preview, and manages connections. [...] Sitemap

Open in app

Sign up

Sign in

. Upon closer inspection, Lattics resembles a more advanced version of Word, or “Word+.”

This design has caused me difficulties because I often confuse Lattics with either note-taking or writing software.It Sucks
